turns.name: CLAIBORNE
turns.text: Whether it is inland or whether it is territorial sea cf the state it would seem to me has little effect on its jurisdictional powers in that a rea. QUESTION; What if it is open sea, though? MR. CLAIBCENE; If it is epen sea, then the state would have no claim tc govern ether than its own citizens within that zone.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Well, there is some of that involved here, I take it. MR. CLAIEORNE; And there is in Block Island Sound some of that involved, and indeed this dispute originally arose because of Ehode Island's claim tc control pilots bringing both domestic and --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Right. Sc that is involved tc that e xtent.

turns.name: CLAIBORNE
turns.text: It is involved to that extent, though it isn't strictly speaking the bone of contention he re. questioning, your brief states that there are international implications, but I don't think they were identified in the brief. What are they, really? ME. CLAIECRNE: Justice Powell, to the extent that there are — that this Court can declare, for instance, that all of Elock Island Sound is territorial water and not high seas, the sea lanes between Block Island and the Rhode Island mainland cn the east and between Elock Island and Long Island on the west would be open to -- would not be open sea, and would net be freely navigated by the world community, but only subject to right of innocent passage and otherwise by permission of the coastal states. And to that extent, to the extent that this Court's decision is viewed because it is an interpretation of international law, the interraticral convention, it would have international radiations. QUESTION; Well, certainly the -- go ahead. QUESTION; Would the position of the United States bring international waters closer to the mainland of the United States? It would to some extent in view of jour position with respect to Block Island Sound, wouldn *t it? ME. CLAIBORNE; That is so, and that is United States to -- freedom of the high seas. QUESTION; May I just follow up? What effect would it have on fishing rights, on electronic surveillance by ether countries, and on mineral rights internationally? ME. CIAIECRNE: With respect to the latter two, Justice Powell, I think I am correct in saying that it would have no effect on the fishing rights or on the right to exploit the subsoil of the sea, because in both these respects the United States has declared for itself ownership of a zone which goes either 2C0 miles cut or to the edge of the continental shelf, and therefore these areas now in dispute would in no event be open to international exploitation cr tc international fishery except by permission or by treaty. With respect to surveillance, I suppose that that would be affected in that so long as these are high seas, any vessel can come in these waters provided it is not for fishing and provided it is not for the purposes of exploiting the seabed. QUESTION; Tc what extent does that work both ways? In other words, I suppose you are concerned -- the government is concerned about a precedent that would involve similar situations in other parts of the world. upon the reason why it has traditionally been the policy of the United States as a matter of international relations to confine the high seas close to shore, relatively close to shore, because of the interest cf the United States in avoiding or wanting to avoid setting a precedent which ether nations can immitate and thereby clcse off the areas near their buoys tc international navigation and as well to our surveillance of their activities. It is as Your Honcr -- QUESTION i The government surely isn’t concerned abcut any navigation problems as between it and the states, is it, because certainly the federal government can regulate to its heart's content in that area.

turns.name: CLAIBORNE
turns.text: I think that is -- Justice Rehnquist. As I have indicated in answer to a question from Justice Shite, it is ccmmcn ground in this case that if Long Island is not an extension of the mainland, then the inland waters behind long Island are restricted to what are known as -- what is known as Long Island Sound proper. If I can refer the Ccurt tc cur exceptions, at the back there are three charts. The first one would proper . On Chart 1, the Court will notice that long Island Sound, the eastern portion of it is indicated, and that it is closed off by a line from Orient Point on the north of the east end of Long Island to Fishers Island and Napa tree Feint. The area west of that is Long Island Sound proper, and it is common ground that that area is inland water net because it satifies the test as an -- tut because it has and always has been historic inland waters of the United States, and that claim by the Unite! States innures to the benefit of the neighboring states, for the most part New York in this case. What is in dispute is the area indicated in Block Island Sound to the east. If Long Island is net an extension of the mainland, no part of Block Island Sound qualifies as part of the bay or as otherwise inland water. There is no claim in this' case at this stage that those waters are historic inland waters. QUESTIONS Kr. Claiborne, what is it about Block Island Sound that the United States does not make it an historic bay, whereas Long Island Sound is in your view? KR. CLAIEORNE: Justice O'Connor, it appears matured as a historic claim, the general rule, although it was net properly speaking a rule, but the tendency was to treat waters which were closable by a line something approaching ten miles only would fit that description, and that line at the north between Orient Point and Fisher Island and Napatree Point is a total of less than ten miles, while a line from Nontauck Point to Washkill or much less a line which would include Block Island would exceed ten miles, and indeed in one case come to 24, or nearby, 24 miles. Accordingly, those waters were not historically viewed as sufficiently enclosed to justify a claim of historic inland water, and as a matter cf fact, the evidence cf 7C piers does not justify such a claim on Block Island Sound, and the two states in whose interest it would have teen to extend the historic claim that far having not sought to do so, presumably for good r eason. New, just to identify the positicr cf the partias further, as the Court sees on this Chart 1 is the position of the United States, that is, long Island not being an extension of the mainland, there is no juridical bay. There is only the historic inland waters cf boundary of inland waters includes no part of Block Island Sound. The only portion of Block Island Sound which we have between the two states are those portions which constitute the three-mile belt which by virtue of the Submerged Lands Act was granted to them in 1953. The special master in this case, having concluded to reach a middle solution, as is perhaps all too often the case, drew a line which — first he concluded that Long Island was an extension of the mainland, and then he closed the bay formed by that island . This is illustrated in the third of our charts. He drew a line between Hontauk Point almost directly north to Washkill Point, and that line enclosed some portion of Block Island Sound. We agree with the master that if Long Island is an extension of the mainland, which we -- that is the correct closing point. Finally, the position of the two states is illustrated in Chart 2. They would first maintain that Long Island is indeed an extension of the mainland, tut thereby by drawing a line net north but east tc Elcck Island, and then a northerly line from Elock Island to Point Judith.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Claiborne, what is the standard of review that this Court traditionally uses in reviewing the findings of fact cf a special master?

turns.name: CLAIBORNE
turns.text: If Your Honor please, the most recent statement by the Court itself with respect tc that matter arises in Colorado versus New Mexico, Number 80 Original, decided June 4th cf this year. In that case, the Court indicated that the master’s findings "reserved respect and a tacit -- I am reading from Page 6 cf the slip opinion -- and a tacit presumption of correctness," but the ultimate responsibility for deciding what are correct findings of fact remains with us . The Court then cites one of its previous decisions in Mississippi versus Arkansas at 415 of the United States Reports, and concludes, "Upon our independent review of the record, we find that Cclcradc has failed to meet its burden." I read these words of the Court as indicating that this Court has concluded that it ought not review a master's findings as though they were the findings of a for matters of credibility where his appraisal of the witnesses must obviously be given more weight than what can be determined cn the cold record, this Court has an obligation to review the evidence to the extent that it is challenged. Here, we say that the Court need go no further than the master's report, because we accept every finding in that report, and so far as I am aware, there is no challenge to any finding of fact in that report by counsel opposite. Accordingly, there is very little occasion in this particular case for the Court to do mere than look at the evidence as summarized by the master. Now, the question whether Long Island is an extension of the mainland, which is the dispositive question in this case, is one that has tended only recently to arise and to arise — It is a troubling development that every island somewhere near the shore is now thought of — for being treated as not an island but a peninsula. If that tendency were to continue unabated, the number of challenges, hence the original docket of this Court would be severely burdened. Now, our brief traces the history of this notion that an island can sometimes be viewed not as an island jurisd ictio nally but as an extension of the mainland mass. It was intended as a very narrow exception to the rule of geography. It was originally applied only in the Louisiana delta and the Mississippi delta in Louisiana, where the land mass is sc criss-crossed ly canals, it looks like a jigsaw puzzle, somewhat separated, and the Court concluded, almost unavoidably, that if one were to take each of the waterways and treat it as separating the mass into islands, Louisiana would wash into the sea. Accordingly, in that special circumstance , it was deemed appropriate, and this was in a case called Louisiana versus Mississippi in 1905, in which this Court said, "To be sure, these are technical islands," speaking of the St. Eernard peninsula, "but we must view it as part of the land mass.” The only other way in which the Court has thought it proper to treat islands as part of the land, part of the land mass, is when usually a small mud lump or ether alluvial formation was appended to the mainland in the sense that it looked like it had dropped off, like a teardrop, and there was a -narrow channel separating it from the mainland.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Mr. Claiborne, may I ask you this S ou nd ? MS. CLAIEORNE; Tbe indications in the record, Justice Powell, are that little trans-Atlantic shipping, mostly coastal shipping, but a very heavy volume of coastal shipping. QUESTION i I know coastal shipping does. MR. CLAIBORNE; And very much navigated. The total volume I have forgotten, but fhe volume is quite substantial, and the channel is some 35 feet deep. QUESTION; Tbirty-five feet? That could accommodate trans-Atlantic shipping. MR. CLAIECRNE; It could indeed. It sc happens that the Pert of New York is mostly to the west rather than on the East River, and therefore the traffic doesn’t go through the East River, and there is no substantial international pert within long Island S c u nd . QUESTION; Mr. Claiborne, may I ask, in your discussion of the islands that you were involved in, what is the government’s position with respect to Manhattan Island? MR. CLAIBORNE; Justice Stevens, we don't have to resolve that question here, because as it happens, whichever way one treats Manhattan, the result is the same The record in this case does indicate that the geographer cf the state — when asked that question in depcsiticn -- he 'died tefore the trial and therefore his deposition was introduced in evidence -- his answer was that Manhattan and indeed Staten Island as well were treated by the committee, the baseline committee which drew the coastline of the United States, as water, as islands and therefore as water because under the rule of Article 7 an island wholly within an indentation is treated as water, tut that is only true with respect to an island, and not with respect to some portion of the land mass. r It is obviously a- closer question whether Staten Island and Manhattan Island are islands for all purposes. The case of long Island is much clearer, because there the separation is some seven-tenths of a mile. Vie have a channel which is a -- channel, not a river, and we have a channel 35 feet deep, and we have it very actively navigated. When one com pares it to an instance which the Court rejected in the Louisiana context, the Eau de Nea and what was claimed to be Calieu Bay, there we ha-. - a channel that was only a third, not even a third of a mile wide, only some 14 feet deep, and carrying a far suppose it would actually be a little tit shorter if it went through long Island Sound. Coes the record shew anything about whether coastal shipping coming frcir east of Block Island goes through the sound on its way to New York or whether it gees south cf long Island? MR. CLAIBORNE; I think, Justice Rehnguist, the record indicates that it does net gc through the sound, that most of that traffic goes -- now, why that should be sc, because I think Ycur Honor is correct that the distance is marginally shorter -- QUESTION; Well, other than perhaps it was just thought not to be a very suitable place to naviga te.

turns.name: CLAIECRNE.
turns.text: And there may be some sense of congestion even in the — QUESTION; Mr. Claiborne, you just a moment ago said that passageway cf seven-tenths cf a mile was heavily navigated. By what?

turns.name: CLAIBORNE
turns.text: 3y commercial vessels.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: From where? Net coastal vessels, or — MR. CLAIECENE; They are coastal vessels, but Boston. They may be bringing goods to and from ports within the sound itself. QUESTION; Sure, and so it is heavily navigated by coastal vessels. SB. CLAIBORNE; It is heavily navigated by commercial coastal vessels, no question about it. QUESTIONi Which have a destination at a pert somewhere on the sound. MR. CLAIEORNE; Yes, and the master's report is quite clear as to the exact volume of that traffic.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But the passageway is navigated by vessels going from New York Harbor to destinations on the sound, or from sound origins into New York? HE. CLAIBORNE; I think in both directions. QUESTION; Yes. HR. CLAIBORNE; Let me say that the master seemed to have recognized that he was straining the rules in treating Long Island, or would be, in treating Long Island — QUESTION; Hr. Claiborne, how many harbors do you have in the sound? HR. CLAIBORNE; Your Honor, I should know that, tut don't. I take it there are Connecticut harbors there as well as some in Rhode Island. MB. CLAlECBNEs I frankly don’t knew, as I volume of traffic indicates that there must be some place to disembark the cargo and to bring it on board. As I said, the master seemed to have recognized the difficulty, especially in light of this Court’s actual rulings and the language in the Louisiana case in treating Long Island which very severely deflects the coast if it is treated as an extension of the mainland, as an extension -- an island. So what he did was to say to himself, what, when I look at what long Island dees, it is clear tc me that what is behind Long Island looks like a bay, and therefore, since it seems tc form a bay, and the only way it can be so legally, I will treat it as an extension of the mainland, totally backward reasoning, result-oriented reasoning. He said this area, Long Island Sound, is used as a bay. It looks like a bay. Therefore it must be a bay, yet it cannot be a bay unless Long Island is an extension of the mainland, therefore I will treat Long Island as an extension of the mainland even though it may not qualify otherwise. Now, that is our bone of contention with the master’s report, and if that scrt of principle, that sort of loose principle were to be encouraged, we would have many more such cases. There are many other states waiting in the wings to see whether this novel effort prevai Is. I will save what time I have.

turns.name: WARREN BURGER
turns.text: Mr. Proudfit. ORAL ARGUMENT CF JCHN G. PRCUDFIT, ESQ., MR. PRCUEFIT; Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, I will respond to the government’s arguments with respect to whether or not there is a bay as found by the master, and Mr. Doherty will address the proper closing lines for that. The master found a juridical bay as he inevitably had to do on the basis of the facts and the law. There were four basic reasons why he found the juridical bay. First of all, the area is geographically a bay. Secondly, it meets all the requirements of Article 7 of the Convention on the Territorial Sea and Contiguous Zone. Thirdly, it satisfies the use and purpose concept in international law, that concept which permits of the mainland. QUESTION! Of course, if you are wrong on the last, your first three reasons evaporate, I take it. HR. PROUDFITi Your Honor, that is correct. As far as this Court gees, that is absolutely correct. First of all, geography. The government wculd have ycu focus on the fact that Long Island is an island. It is surrounded by water. The states have never disputed that. Eut the reality is that that island forms a bay, forms a very large bay, encloses these waters. It is a unique island. It is a unique bay . The government referred to the charts in the back of their brief. Ycu will notice that those charts only show you the Block Island Sound area. Now, I submit that from their point of view, that is what they would like to have you see. From our point of view, and from reality, we wculd like ycu first of all geographically tc lock at Long Island in tote and Long Island Sound. In the back of the master's report, at Appendix E, there is a chart which shews you that area. have provided ycu with an additional map, a satellite map that you have before you, and that map, if you care to pursue it at some pcint, will give ycu a better idea because of the contrast in the cclcr -- QUESTION; Is this the one, Mr. Froudfit? MR. EFCUDFIT; That's correct, Ycur Honor. What I would suggest the first thing that you see geographically that Long Island fits into the mainland and in fact geographically is closer to the mainland than the government would suggest. It is separated from the mainland by a body of water referred to as the East River. And because it is separated by that body of water, the government says it is — may not be considered as closely related to the mainland. New, this Port of Louisiana case suggested factors which should be considered in determining whether an island is realistically part of the mainland. The first of these factors is size. Long Island is a very large island. It is over 100 miles long. It has 450 miles of shoreline. It contains four counties of the State of New York. It contains two boroughs of the State of New York. Over half the city population is on that island.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: So it is two boroughs and Nassau and Suffolk County?

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: Yes, that is correct. Now, the only -- I think the relevant thing about size in relationship to the water that is enclosed, it is in relationship to the projection from the mainland, and I might also say that size is another consideration when the government talks about islands and mud lumps in the Mississippi — in the delta area and in the Gulf. I would say tc you that in my opinion, there is absolutely no comparison. The master looked at charts of that area. He locked at charts of islands in Alaska, and there was no comparison. The next factor which this Court said should be considered is distance from the mainland. New, the East River separates Long Island from the mainland --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: What are ycu talking about when you refer tc the mainland?

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Manhattan?

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: I am talking about Manhattan, don’t think there would be any dispute about that being the mainland. QUESTION; Well, when the government says that the East River is seven-tenths of a mile wide, where is it measuring the East River? MR. PROUEFIT; That, Your Honor, is measured from Frog’s Neck to Willet's Point, and that is net the narrowest point. Tf you went straight across — QUESTION; Frog's Neck to — that is just the north end of Flushing? MR. PROUEFIT; That’s correct. Ycur Honor -- QUESTION; I've get it. That is the two-thirds of a mile. MR. PROUDFIT; Yes. QUESTION; Seven-tenths of a mile. Hew wide is it down at Manhattan? MR. PROUEFIT; Well, it gets down to about three-tenths of a mile. QUESTION; On either side of — what’s the island there in — MR. PROUDFIT; That would be — QUESTION; Rikers Island? MR. PROUDFIT; It slips my — QUESTION; On your map the very narrowest QUESTIONS And how wide is the water passage the re?

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: Well, it would be less than three-tenths of a mile, Your Honor, but I think it gets even narrower farther down. QUESTIONS Under the convention, dc you have to treat islands like that as water, however?

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: Your Honor —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I mean, you are not just — isn’t that what the Convention instructs us to dc? ME. FRCUDFITs Justice O'Conner, it says to you, treat islands as water in the terms of the measurement of water area for complying with the semicircle test. Mr. Claiborne suggested that the geographer of the United States, Dr. Hodgson, treated that as water. In fact, Exhibit 78 to the deposition — I am sorry. It is Exhibit 73. U.S. 73 is the deposition of Dr. Hodgson. Cn Page 78 he says that Manhattan would be considered a part of the mainland, and obviously you can see the connection there, the river between the two —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: But not Roosevelt Island? mainland, is Roosevelt Island? HR. PROUDFIT: Yotr Hcncr, Rccsevelt Island is an island. We don’t consider that —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Nc matter what you do atout Long Island, it is an island? HE. PROUDFIT: There is not a bridge running over there.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It doesn’t make any difference anyway ? HR. FRCUBFIT: It doesn't make any difference at all. Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: The next factor is the depth and utility of the intervening waters.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Are you sure there is no bridge to Roosevelt Island?

turns.name: PROUDFIT
turns.text: Your Honor, you caught me cut. The Queensborc Eridge goes acrcss Rccsevelt Island. You are exactly right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: I was just getting ready to blew my brains out. fact there is a tram car that runs from Manhattan, tut are exactly right. The depth and utility of the intervening waters, the waters of the East River are internal waters. New, basically the East River is a strait running between two bays. It runs between New Ycrk Harbor and it runs between the waters of Long Island Sound. That opening into the East River at the end cf Long Island Sound is dwarfed by the size of the water body. It is dwarfed by the size cf the island. The depth, as Mr. Claiborne suggested to you, is 34 feet, and that depth has teen increased by a major project of the Corps of Engineers back in the 1980's. Shape is another factor. It is a long projection from the mainland enclosing a large tody cf water. QUESTION i Is that East River body of water substantially greater than that of other channels which this Court has previously ignored for purposes of determining a headland? MR. PROUDFITi Your Honor, I think it probably is. It certainly is deeper, but I wculd suggest tc lour Hcncr that that --

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Greater navigability and deeper MR. PROUDFITi That is correct, Your Honor, and I would suggest that that is relative here to the size of the island and to the size of the water bodies. There are also here, of course, physical connections to the mainland . There are the — QUESTIONi Do you really think, that we should follow a principle that says because you build a bridge you ought to be able to annex anisland? Doesn’t that have international ramifications that we might net like as a nation? MR. PRCUDFITi Your Honor, I — QUESTIONi Under — in interpreting the Geneva Con ven tion ? MR. PROUDFITi Justice O'Connor, I would suggest that that is one factor that this Court considered. These are. major physical connections. QUESTIONi Sell, isn’t^that kind of a risky factor on which to determine this question? MR. PROUDFITi Your Honor, that is one additional factor that I would suggest, and I certainly wouldn’t hinge the relationship on that as the only factor . QUESTION! You think you can win even without that factor. say that we can win because of the close re latio nship between Long Island and the mainland that is geographically there, that is there by common sense, and is there because of the other factors which this Court -- which I have enumerated, and because the Fast River in separating long Island from the mainland is -- according to the government, the only reason that long Island Scund can’t be a bay is because that is net a river. None of those factors would change if in fact that was a river, so that is their distinction, and what I say is, it is not a river, but it is an estuary, it is a very complex estuary, which their own witness, Dr. Swanson, testified to, and the Hudson River is an estuary. The Convention would consider obviously estuaries in terms of bays, because we are talking about areas that are cn the ccean, but let me just — QUESTION; Could I — don’t you think the fact that the government agrees, the special master found and the government agrees that this is a historic bay, doesn’t that support ycur argument that Leng Island is part of the mainland? MR. PROUDFITs Your Honor, if you consider have — on occasion people have said that they recognize it as a historic bay. They only recognize it as historic waters. If in fact they recognize it as a historic bay, then they would have to go the other mile and say it was a juridical bay. But I think this Court has said the best and most workable definition of a hay is the Convention, and the Convention, all the requirements of the Convention were found by the master to apply to this area. Also, the whole concept in international law which permits a bay to be closed is the fact that it is an area that is surrounded by land, it is closely related to that land, and the reason it can be closed off is because by closing it off you are not interfering with through international traffic going between two foreign states . And the master found that that was the situation here, that there was no through foreign commerce passing through this area. For all the reasons I have suggested plus common sense, Long Island forms a bay. The master said if there was ever a situation where a large island would be considered a part of the mainland, so the waters enclosed between the island and the coast can be a Mr. Chief Justice, if I may. ON EEHAIF OF RHODE ISLAND MR. DOHERTYs Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court, this is net a case of Rhode Island imperialism. lie are certainly happy to be the smallest state, and we are happy to remain as such. However, we do not wish to have an Argo Merchant accident on our shores . And because the Congress at the very beginning of this Republic in the law now codified as 46 U.S. Code, Section 211, saw fit to delegate to the various states the responsibility or the obligation if they so chose to regulate pilotage in their waters and in the bays, inlets, and harbors of the United States then they could sc do. And Rhode Island has chosen to do that, and that is the genesis of this litigation we have here today. A question earlier from the bench certainly made reference to Hall versus Dunlap, which is pending on certiorari before this Court in Docket Number 75-699C. And should we lose this case, should Block Island Sound and Long Island Sound not be found by this longer be able to regulate pilotage of foreign flag vessels and American vessels under foreign registry in Block Island Sound. This is not a small item. Supertankers navigate through Block Island Sound and into long Island Sound, passing between the Rhode Island coast and Block Island. They go to Fort Jefferson, where there is a major offload facility. Other smaller tankers, because of the draft in the East River, go down and dock and unload in the East River in the vicinity of LaGuardia Airport and those facilities there. So, we are talking about a very serious issue to Rhode Island. Further -- QUESTION i You say they gc through Block Island Sound — MR. DOHERTY! That's correct. QUESTION! -- and then go down — come up through the narrows?

turns.name: DOHERTY
turns.text: No, they go through Block Island Sound, Justice Rehnquist. They then go through the race, which is between Fishers Island —

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: DOHERTY
turns.text: — and a number of small islands, Little Gull Island and Great Gull Island, and Island, which is on the north coast of long Island, about midway down.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And that is their point of destination ? HE. DOHERTY: That is their point of destination. They also go to New Haven Harbor. There are also major oil facilities there. The smaller tankers go to Shelter Harbor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: How about New London? HE. DOHERTY: There are smaller facilities in New London, not as large as in New Haven, Justice Whi te.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: This is traffic from -- foreign traffic headed for ports on the sound then. HR. DOHERTY: On the sound.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: And it goes through Block Island Sound.

turns.name: DOHERTY
turns.text: There is — Captain Neery was a Rhode Island witness, and there was testimony also from the United States, Dr. Swanson, I believe, which spoke of vessel movements. The predominant vessels that enter Block Island Sound, they go to ports on Long Island Sound and they go only as far as the East River. They don’t navigate through the East Fiver and New Jersey ports, Bayonne and the like. These vessels come into lower New York Bay. So that we are not talking about through traffic that is going up the Hudson River. They go outside. Sc that the international usage cf these waters is just not there. It is not there in the record, and I don't think the international implication which you referred to, Justice Fowell, are there in the record . This scepter that is raised in this sort cf litigation, the impact of fisheries, the impact cf coastal zone management is just not there in this case. Turning to Block Island Sound, and accepting for the moment New Ycrk's argument and Rhode Island’s argument that long Island is an extension cf the mainland, the question becomes, how do you close that area of water known as Elcck Island Sound, and I wculd refer the Ccurt to the master's repert, which should be in Appendix C, which contains a chart of the area and the master’s outlines cf various alternatives presented to him . As I did in my brief, I would say that the solicitor has characterized the master's report as one of seeking middle ground. Rhode Island would sc Article 7. Article 7, Subsection 5 and 4 contemplate that under Article 7 that the nation should close the largest amount of water possible under those rules, and the line to Watch Hill Point is certainly a probable line, and it is less than 24 miles, and the area behind it is greater than a semicircle of its length. Eut Weekapaug Point, which is slightly tc the east, also satisfies that test. QUESTION; Which one would you draw? MR. DOHERTY; We draw the line from Montauk Point to Point Judith. We point out that the Weekapaug Point line is a correct one if you choose not to draw a line tc Block Island. QUESTION; But wouldn't that line be longer than 24 miles? MR. DOHERTY; Not the line tc Weekapaug Point. So, Justice C’Ccnnor. QUESTION; No, but the line from Montauk tc Pcir.t Judith? MR. DCHEPTY; The line itself from Montauk Point tc Point Judith would be slightly in excess of 24 miles QUESTION; And that would violate the Convention. MR. And upon that, we submit that Block Island Sound and Long Island Sound should be viewed together, and lines should be drawn as Rhode Island posits them and as New York posits them.
